one_line_takeaway: PragLocker obfuscates an LLM agent's system prompt via black-box code-symbolization plus random-search noise injection so it keeps working on the deploying LLM (1.01x utility) but collapses to near-zero on any other LLM (0.2x aggregate portability vs 1.0x unprotected), and resists deobfuscation/self-prompting attacks (1.03x-4.78x recovery) except on code-generation tasks where LLM-assisted recovery still reaches 82-87% of original performance.
key_finding: Without protection, prompts are highly portable across LLMs (e.g., a HumanEval prompt scores 98.78 on Gemini 2 and 97.56 on DeepSeek when copied from another target). Full PragLocker drives cross-model reuse to near-zero on many tasks (e.g., 3.04/1.22 on HumanEval when the GPT-4o-targeted prompt is copied to Gemini 2/DeepSeek; near-zero scores of 0.04-0.14 on DialSim/LoCoMo) and achieves the lowest aggregate Mean Portability Loss ratio of 0.2x versus 1.00x for no protection, 0.82x for PragLocker_tune (optimization-only), and 0.99x for PragLocker_code (code-symbol-only) -- demonstrating that neither stage alone is sufficient and both are needed together. On the target LLM itself, utility is preserved or slightly improved: Performance Preservation is 1.01x relative to the unprotected prompt (e.g., HumanEval on GPT-4o goes from 93.90 to 94.51; QuALITY on DeepSeek goes from 88.06 to 87.97). Adaptive attacks are largely ineffective for most tasks: the Deobfuscation attack (attacker knows the exact pipeline and objective) only reaches a 1.03x Relative Attack Gain, weaker than a naive self-prompting baseline (4.78x) where the attacker simply asks the target LLM to synthesize a fresh prompt from input-output pairs -- implying attackers do better ignoring PragLocker's structure entirely than trying to invert it. However, LLM-assisted recovery (querying the target LLM to reconstruct a natural-language version of the stolen prompt) is notably more effective specifically on the code-generation tasks: it recovers 82-87% pass@1 on HumanEval/MBPP (vs. near-zero for the raw stolen prompt and ~94-98% for the true unprotected original), while remaining much weaker on QA/memory tasks (e.g., NarrativeQA recovery of ~5-7 F1 vs. near-zero raw and ~20-25 original), yielding an overall aggregate Relative Attack Gain of 3.49x across all tasks. Intra-family portability within Qwen2.5 (7B/14B/4-bit) shows no qualitative improvement in attacker success versus inter-family transfer (e.g., NarrativeQA original scores of 16.21/19.36/18.98 drop to only 5.96-8.97 under cross-sibling reuse). Token-only feedback (no log-probabilities, 100-epoch budget) still preserves target utility (85.85%/71.31%/82.05% QuALITY accuracy on GPT-4o/Gemini 2/DeepSeek respectively) while showing clear cross-model degradation (e.g., the GPT-4o-optimized prompt drops from 85.85% to 58.16% on Gemini 2 and 63.50% on DeepSeek).
core_contribution: PragLocker is a black-box scheme for protecting LLM-agent system prompts as intellectual property when the agent is deployed in an untrusted environment (third-party hosting, multi-tenant cloud, or end-user device). It replaces the deployer's plaintext system prompt with an obfuscated prompt that (1) preserves the agent's task performance when run on the target LLM but (2) collapses in performance when copied and reused on a different LLM, so a stolen prompt cannot be repurposed by an attacker. The method has two phases: (i) Prompt Initialization, where the target LLM itself is prompted to re-encode the original natural-language prompt into a non-natural-language 'code-symbol' (DSL-like) form that preserves semantics but is no longer plain text; (ii) Non-Portable Obfuscation Optimization, a gradient-free random-search procedure that injects character-level noise into the code-symbol prompt and accepts a mutation only if it lowers a combined loss L = L_task + lambda*L_dist + gamma*L_non-lang (task cross-entropy via target-LLM log-probabilities, negative sigmoid of Levenshtein distance from the original prompt, and negative Shannon entropy of the prompt's character distribution). The paper also gives a proof-sketch existence theorem (Theorem 3.2, deferred to an appendix) arguing that, because transformer output margins are continuous functions of input embeddings, there exist locally-stable embedding perturbations that preserve a target model's greedy-decoding output while being unlikely to fall inside a different model's distinct stability region, motivating why model-specific non-portable prompts should exist.
threat_model: Two parties: a Defender who deploys an LLM agent as a service and accesses the underlying foundation LLM only through a black-box API (no gradients, no model internals) -- the target LLM itself is trusted not to exfiltrate prompts, but the deployment environment (hosting platform, multi-tenant infra, or end-user device) is untrusted; and an Adversary (e.g., malicious end user or insider cloud operator) who can physically obtain the deployed prompt artifact and wants to reuse it to build a comparable agent on a different, possibly stronger, proprietary LLM. The adversary is also allowed adaptive capabilities beyond naive copying: (a) LLM-assisted recovery, querying the target LLM itself to reconstruct a natural-language version of the obfuscated prompt; (b) a deobfuscation attack, where the attacker knows PragLocker's exact objective and iteratively re-optimizes the stolen prompt with the distance and non-language terms inverted to push it back toward natural language, given the same task data and metrics as the defender; and (c) a naive self-prompting baseline, where the target LLM is asked to synthesize a fresh prompt directly from task input-output examples (i.e., an attacker bypassing recovery altogether). This is a prompt-confidentiality / anti-piracy threat model, not an adversarial-input or jailbreak threat model -- the paper does not address prompt injection, data exfiltration via the agent's outputs, or malicious inputs to the deployed agent.
limitations: The paper's own Limitation and Discussion section (Section 5) states: (1) Tokenizer Compatibility -- character-level noise injection requires the target LLM's tokenizer to have broad character coverage so perturbed text maps to valid subword/byte tokens rather than being rejected as unknown tokens; this generally holds for mainstream production API LLMs (GPT-series, Gemini) but is not guaranteed universally. (2) Prompt-Length Overhead -- the obfuscation process can lengthen the system prompt and increase prefill cost, though the authors argue this is amortized because the obfuscated prompt is a fixed, cacheable KV prefix. (3) No comparison against white-box prompt-protection methods (e.g., representation-space obfuscation requiring model-internal access) because those operate under different assumptions not directly comparable to the black-box setting. (4) Protection scope is explicitly limited to deployed prompts as IP; the paper does not claim to protect other agent IP components (e.g., tool implementations, orchestration logic) or defend against threats outside prompt theft (prompt injection, jailbreaking, data exfiltration via agent behavior are out of scope). Additionally, the LLM-assisted recovery results reveal an asymmetry the authors report but do not fully resolve: recovery is markedly more effective on structured code-generation tasks (82-87% pass@1 recovered) than on open-ended QA/memory tasks, suggesting the non-portability guarantee is weaker for tasks with highly templated/constrained outputs where an attacker's reconstructed natural-language prompt can approximate the original well enough. The theoretical existence argument (Theorem 3.2) is explicitly caveated by the authors themselves ('although our methodology is not entirely aligned with our discussions above... these analyses serve as a theoretical lens') as motivational rather than a rigorous guarantee that the actual random-search algorithm converges to such prompts.

quantitative_results: Unprotected cross-model portability: HumanEval prompt scores 98.78 (Gemini 2) and 97.56 (DeepSeek) when copied from another target LLM
PragLocker cross-model portability on same setting: 3.04 (Gemini 2) / 1.22 (DeepSeek)
Aggregate Mean Portability Loss ratio: Without protection 1.00x; PragLocker (full) 0.20x; PragLocker_tune (optimization-only) 0.82x; PragLocker_code (code-symbol-only) 0.99x
Performance Preservation on target LLM (Table 3): aggregate ratio 1.01x vs 1.00x unprotected (e.g., HumanEval GPT-4o 93.90 to 94.51; QuALITY DeepSeek 88.06 to 87.97)
Adaptive-attack Relative Attack Gain (Table 5): PragLocker baseline 1x; Deobfuscation attack 1.03x; LLM-assisted recovery 3.49x; Naive self-prompting 4.78x
LLM-assisted recovery on code tasks: HumanEval recovers to 86.59 (Gemini 2) / 87.20 (DeepSeek), MBPP recovers to ~83-84, versus raw stolen-prompt scores of ~0.6-3.0 and true original ~93-98
Intra-family (Qwen2.5) portability: NarrativeQA original 16.21 (7B) / 19.36 (14B) / 18.98 (14B-4bit) drops to 5.96-8.97 under cross-sibling reuse

Token-only feedback variant (100 epochs, QuALITY): target accuracy 85.85% (GPT-4o) / 71.31% (Gemini 2) / 82.05% (DeepSeek), with cross-model drop to 58.16%/63.50% for the GPT-4o-optimized prompt on Gemini 2/DeepSeek
baselines_compared: PragLocker_tune (optimization stage only, no code-symbol initialization)
Deobfuscation attack (inverted-objective re-optimization by an adaptive attacker)
LLM-assisted recovery (target LLM reconstructs natural-language prompt from obfuscated one)
Naive self-prompting (target LLM synthesizes a fresh prompt from task input-output pairs)
